name: Masterpieces: Who Painted It?
description: 99 of the most famous paintings in Western and world art history, from the Mona Lisa to the Qingming scroll. See the work, name the artist; details give title, year, and movement.

fields.extra: Mona Lisa (c. 1503-1506), High Renaissance. Louvre, Paris; the world's most famous portrait.
The Starry Night (1889), Post-Impressionism. Painted from his asylum window at Saint-Remy.
The Scream (1893), Expressionism. Munch described hearing an infinite scream passing through nature.
Girl with a Pearl Earring (c. 1665), Dutch Golden Age. Often called the Mona Lisa of the North; Mauritshuis, The Hague.
The Last Supper (c. 1495-1498), High Renaissance. Mural in the refectory of Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan.
The Creation of Adam (c. 1512), High Renaissance. Fresco on the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel.
The Birth of Venus (c. 1485), Early Renaissance. Venus arrives at the shore on a scallop shell; Uffizi, Florence.
The Great Wave off Kanagawa (c. 1831), ukiyo-e. Woodblock print from Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
American Gothic (1930), Regionalism. The models were the artist's sister and his dentist.
The Kiss (1907-1908), Vienna Secession. The high point of Klimt's gold leaf Golden Phase.
Arrangement in Grey and Black No. 1 (1871), Realism. Popularly known as Whistler's Mother.
Las Meninas (1656), Baroque. The painter included himself at his easel; Prado, Madrid.
The Night Watch (1642), Dutch Golden Age. Militia group portrait; Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam.
Impression, Sunrise (1872), Impressionism. Its title gave the Impressionist movement its name.
A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte (1884-1886), Neo-Impressionism. The defining work of pointillism.
The Garden of Earthly Delights (c. 1490-1510), Northern Renaissance. Triptych moving from Eden to a nightmarish hell.
Liberty Leading the People (1830), Romanticism. Commemorates the July Revolution of 1830 in Paris.
Wanderer above the Sea of Fog (c. 1818), Romanticism. The defining image of the Romantic sublime.
The School of Athens (1509-1511), High Renaissance. Vatican fresco with Plato and Aristotle at its center.
Sunflowers (1888), Post-Impressionism. Painted in Arles to decorate the room awaiting Gauguin.
Café Terrace at Night (1888), Post-Impressionism. A night scene painted entirely without black.
The Arnolfini Portrait (1434), Early Netherlandish. The convex mirror reflects the entire scene in miniature.
The Milkmaid (c. 1658), Dutch Golden Age. Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam.
The Third of May 1808 (1814), Romanticism. French troops execute Madrid civilians after an uprising.
Olympia (1863), Realism. Its frank gaze scandalized the Paris Salon of 1865.
Luncheon of the Boating Party (1881), Impressionism. Friends of the artist at a restaurant terrace on the Seine.
Bridge over a Pond of Water Lilies (1899), Impressionism. From the water garden the artist built at Giverny.
Self-Portrait (1889), Post-Impressionism. Painted at Saint-Remy; Musée d'Orsay, Paris.
The Raft of the Medusa (1818-1819), Romanticism. Survivors of the wrecked French frigate Méduse.
Primavera (c. 1482), Early Renaissance. Allegory of spring with Venus, Flora, and the Three Graces.
Napoleon Crossing the Alps (1801), Neoclassicism. In reality Napoleon made the crossing on a mule.
The Death of Marat (1793), Neoclassicism. The revolutionary journalist stabbed in his bath by Charlotte Corday.
Saturn Devouring His Son (c. 1819-1823), Romanticism. One of the Black Paintings from the walls of Goya's house.
The Gleaners (1857), Realism. Peasant women gathering leftover grain after the harvest.
The Hay Wain (1821), Romanticism. A cart crossing the River Stour in Suffolk.
The Fighting Temeraire (1839), Romanticism. A veteran of Trafalgar towed away to be scrapped.
Ophelia (1851-1852), Pre-Raphaelite. Model Elizabeth Siddal posed in a bathtub for months.
The Lady of Shalott (1888), Pre-Raphaelite style. Based on Tennyson's poem of the cursed weaver.
Washington Crossing the Delaware (1851), history painting. The 1776 surprise attack on Trenton, painted in Dusseldorf.
The Sleeping Gypsy (1897), Naive art. A lion sniffs at a sleeping traveler under a full moon.
Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going? (1897-1898), Post-Impressionism. Painted in Tahiti as Gauguin's testament.
The Card Players (1890s), Post-Impressionism. One of a series of five versions.
At the Moulin Rouge (1892-1895), Post-Impressionism. Montmartre nightlife; the artist appears in the background.
A Bar at the Folies-Bergère (1882), Impressionism. The artist's last major work; note the puzzling mirror reflection.
Bal du moulin de la Galette (1876), Impressionism. Sunday afternoon dancing in Montmartre.
The Dance Class (1874), Impressionism. Ballet master Jules Perrot leads the class.
The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp (1632), Dutch Golden Age. The commission that made the young Rembrandt's name.
Venus of Urbino (1538), Venetian Renaissance. Uffizi, Florence.
Sistine Madonna (c. 1512-1513), High Renaissance. The two bored cherubs at its base became famous on their own.
The Last Judgment (1536-1541), High Renaissance. Fresco covering the altar wall of the Sistine Chapel.
The Tower of Babel (1563), Northern Renaissance. Modeled partly on the Colosseum; Vienna.
The Hunters in the Snow (1565), Northern Renaissance. From a series depicting the months of the year.
The Ambassadors (1533), Northern Renaissance. The smear across the floor is an anamorphic skull.
Lady with an Ermine (c. 1489-1491), High Renaissance. Portrait of Cecilia Gallerani; Krakow.
The Calling of Saint Matthew (1599-1600), Baroque. A shaft of light picks Matthew out of the tax office.
Judith Slaying Holofernes (c. 1614-1620), Baroque. Gentileschi's unflinching take on the biblical scene.
Portrait of Innocent X (1650), Baroque. Later the basis for Francis Bacon's screaming popes.
The Return of the Prodigal Son (c. 1668), Dutch Golden Age. A late masterpiece; Hermitage, Saint Petersburg.
The Goldfinch (1654), Dutch Golden Age. The painter died that year in the Delft gunpowder explosion.
Laughing Cavalier (1624), Dutch Golden Age. Wallace Collection, London; he is neither laughing nor a cavalier.
View of Delft (c. 1660-1661), Dutch Golden Age. Proust called it the most beautiful painting in the world.
The Swing (1767), Rococo. A hidden admirer watches from the bushes below.
The Blue Boy (c. 1770), British portraiture. An homage to Van Dyck; Huntington Library, California.
The Nightmare (1781), Romanticism. An incubus crouches on a sleeping woman.
La Grande Odalisque (1814), Neoclassicism. Criticized for the anatomically impossible elongated back.
Oath of the Horatii (1784), Neoclassicism. Three brothers swear to defend Rome.
Rain, Steam and Speed (1844), Romanticism. A Great Western Railway train crosses the Maidenhead bridge.
The Ninth Wave (1850), Romanticism. Shipwreck survivors cling to wreckage at sunrise.
The Kiss (1859), Italian Romanticism. Became a symbol of the Risorgimento.
A Burial at Ornans (1849-1850), Realism. An ordinary village funeral painted at heroic scale.
Barge Haulers on the Volga (1870-1873), Realism. Eleven laborers drag a barge upstream.
Morning in a Pine Forest (1889), Realism. The bears were painted by Konstantin Savitsky.
Isle of the Dead (1880), Symbolism. A shrouded figure is rowed toward a sepulchral island.
Flaming June (1895), Victorian Academic art. Rediscovered in the 1960s; Museo de Arte de Ponce, Puerto Rico.
Portrait of Madame X (1884), society portraiture. The fallen shoulder strap scandalized the Paris Salon.
The Gulf Stream (1899), Realism. A lone sailor adrift among sharks.
Paris Street; Rainy Day (1877), Impressionism. Haussmann's new Paris in the rain.
Woman with a Parasol (1875), Impressionism. Camille Monet and son Jean on a breezy hillside.
The Child's Bath (1893), Impressionism. Composition influenced by Japanese prints.
The Cradle (1872), Impressionism. Shown at the first Impressionist exhibition in 1874.
The Basket of Apples (c. 1893), Post-Impressionism. Its deliberately tilted perspective anticipated Cubism.
The Yellow Christ (1889), Post-Impressionism. Key work of Cloisonnism, painted in Brittany.
The Potato Eaters (1885), early realist period. Painted in Nuenen before his palette brightened in Paris.
Bedroom in Arles (1888), Post-Impressionism. His room in the Yellow House; three versions exist.
Irises (1889), Post-Impressionism. Painted in the asylum garden at Saint-Remy; Getty Museum.
Wheatfield with Crows (1890), Post-Impressionism. One of his final paintings at Auvers.
Tiger in a Tropical Storm (Surprised!) (1891), Naive art. The artist's first jungle painting.
Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer I (1907), Vienna Secession. The Woman in Gold, restituted to the family's heirs in 2006.
Composition VII (1913), abstract art. Kandinsky called it the most complex work he ever painted.
Black Square (1915), Suprematism. First shown hung across a corner, like a Russian icon.
Composition with Red, Blue and Yellow (1930), De Stijl. Neoplasticism reduced to primaries and grid lines.
Dance (1910), Fauvist modernism. Hermitage, Saint Petersburg.
Self-Portrait with Physalis (1912), Expressionism. Leopold Museum, Vienna.
Along the River During the Qingming Festival (early 12th century), Song dynasty handscroll. Panorama of daily life in the capital Bianjing.
Self-Portrait at Twenty-Eight (1500), Northern Renaissance. A strikingly Christ-like frontal pose.
Young Hare (1502), Northern Renaissance. A watercolor of astonishing precision; Albertina, Vienna.
View of Toledo (c. 1596-1600), Mannerism. One of the first pure landscapes in Spanish art.
The Burial of the Count of Orgaz (1586), Mannerism. Church of Santo Tome, Toledo.
The Descent from the Cross (1612-1614), Flemish Baroque. Central panel of the triptych in Antwerp Cathedral.
